front: Affordance
back:
Definition

A design property that signals how an object can be used. Coined by psychologist James Gibson (1977) and popularized in design by Don Norman in The Design of Everyday Things (1988).

Two Types

  • Perceived affordance — what the user thinks they can do based on visual cues
  • Real affordance — what the object actually allows

Good design aligns both. When they diverge, users get confused or frustrated.

Examples in Digital Design

Common affordances
  • A raised button with a shadow affords pressing — the depth cue says "push me"
  • A scrollbar affords dragging — its shape and position signal movement
  • Underlined blue text affords clicking — learned convention from the early web
  • A text field with a blinking cursor affords typing
  • A draggable handle (⠿) affords reordering

Physical World Examples

Everyday objects
  • A flat plate on a door → push. A handle → pull.
  • A chair's flat seat → sit. Its back → lean.
  • Scissors' holes → insert fingers. Blades → cut.

Why It Matters

When affordances are wrong, users need instructions. A door that requires a "PUSH" sign has failed affordance — the design itself should communicate the action. The same principle applies to interfaces: if you need a tooltip to explain a button, the affordance is weak.

Design Insight

Flat design trends removed many visual affordances (shadows, bevels, gradients). This led to "mystery meat" navigation where users couldn't tell what was clickable. Modern design balances minimalism with enough visual cues to guide interaction.

front: Heuristic Evaluation
back:
What It Is

A usability inspection method where evaluators examine an interface against recognized design principles — without involving users.

Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (1994)

  1. Visibility of system status — always show what's happening
  2. Match between system & real world — use familiar language
  3. User control and freedom — easy undo, clear exits
  4. Consistency and standards — don't reinvent conventions
  5. Error prevention — stop mistakes before they happen
  6. Recognition over recall — show options, don't make users remember
  7. Flexibility and efficiency — shortcuts for power users
  8. Aesthetic and minimalist design — every element earns its place
  9. Help users recover from errors — clear, actionable error messages
  10. Help and documentation — easy to search, task-focused

How to Run One

Step-by-step process
  1. Recruit 3-5 evaluators (designers, PMs, or devs with UX awareness)
  2. Each person reviews the interface independently
  3. They flag issues and tag which heuristic is violated
  4. Rate severity: 0 (not a problem) to 4 (usability catastrophe)
  5. Combine findings and prioritize by severity × frequency

Why 3-5 Evaluators?

1 evaluator → finds ~35% of issues 3 evaluators → finds ~60% of issues 5 evaluators → finds ~75% of issues 10 evaluators → diminishing returns

Nielsen's research shows the sweet spot is 3-5 people. More than that yields diminishing returns relative to the cost.

When to Use It

Early in design (before user testing), after redesigns, or when you can't access real users. It's fast, cheap, and catches obvious problems — but it's not a substitute for real user research.

front: Double Diamond
back:
Overview

A design process framework by the UK Design Council (2005). It visualizes the creative process as two diamonds — alternating between expanding possibilities and narrowing focus.

The Shape

DISCOVER DEFINE DEVELOP DELIVER ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ◆ ╲ ╱ ◆ ╲ ╱ ◆ ╲ ╱ ◆ ╲ diverge converge diverge converge ─────────────┬──────────────┬────────── Problem │ Solution │ Space │ Space │

Four Phases

  • Discover (diverge) — research broadly, talk to users, explore the problem space without assumptions. Cast a wide net.
  • Define (converge) — synthesize research into a clear problem statement. Narrow from "everything we learned" to "the one thing we're solving."
  • Develop (diverge) — ideate many possible solutions. Sketches, wireframes, crazy ideas. Quantity over quality at this stage.
  • Deliver (converge) — test, refine, and ship the strongest solution. Iterate based on feedback until it's ready.

The Key Insight

Why teams fail

Most teams skip the first diamond entirely. They jump straight from a stakeholder request to designing solutions — without ever validating that they're solving the right problem. The Double Diamond forces you to understand before you solve.

In Practice

Example: redesigning checkout
  • Discover: Interview 10 users, analyze drop-off analytics, review support tickets
  • Define: "Users abandon checkout because they can't estimate shipping costs upfront"
  • Develop: Sketch 5 different approaches (calculator, flat rate, free threshold, etc.)
  • Deliver: Prototype top 2, A/B test, ship the winner

front: North Star Metric
back:
Definition

The single metric that best captures the core value your product delivers to customers. It aligns every team around what matters most and serves as a compass for prioritization.

Examples by Company

Real-world North Stars
  • Airbnb → Nights booked
  • Spotify → Time spent listening
  • Facebook → Daily active users
  • Slack → Messages sent per team per week
  • Netflix → Hours of content watched
  • Uber → Rides per week

What Makes a Good North Star

  • Reflects customer value, not just revenue
  • Is measurable and trackable over time
  • Leads to sustainable growth when improved
  • Is something every team can influence
  • Is a leading indicator of business health

The Input Tree

North Star Metric ╱ │ ╲ Input 1 Input 2 Input 3 (acq.) (activation) (retention) │ │ │ Team A Team B Team C

Break your North Star into input metrics that different teams own. This gives each team a clear contribution to the bigger picture.

Common Mistakes

Vanity metrics to avoid
  • Page views — high traffic ≠ value delivered
  • Downloads — installed ≠ retained
  • Registered users — signed up ≠ engaged

These look good in reports but don't correlate with real product health.

front: Information Architecture (IA)
back:
Definition

The structural design of shared information environments — how content is organized, labeled, and navigated so users can find what they need.

Four Core Components

  • Organization schemes — how items are grouped (alphabetical, by topic, by task, by audience)
  • Labeling systems — what things are called (navigation labels, category names, button text)
  • Navigation systems — how users move through content (global nav, breadcrumbs, filters)
  • Search systems — how users find specific things (search bar, autocomplete, faceted search)

Common IA Methods

Research techniques
  • Card sorting — users group content into categories that make sense to them. Reveals mental models.
  • Tree testing — users find items in a proposed hierarchy. Tests if your structure works without visual design distracting.
  • Site mapping — visual diagram of page structure and relationships. Shows the big picture.

Mental Model Diagram

User's Mental Model Your IA Structure ───────────────── ───────────────── How they think How you built it about the content Alignment = ✅ easy to use Mismatch = ❌ "I can't find anything"

Why It Matters

IA is rooted in library science. Bad IA means users can't find what they need — no matter how beautiful the UI is. You can have the best visual design in the world, but if content is organized poorly, users will leave frustrated.

Real-World Example

E-commerce IA challenge

Should "wireless earbuds" live under Electronics → Audio, or Accessories → Wearables? Card sorting with 20 users showed 75% expected Audio. That's your answer — match the user's mental model, not your org chart.

front: How to explain a design trade-off to stakeholders
back:
The Framework

Structure trade-offs as decisions with evidence, not preferences.

Template

"We considered [Option A] and [Option B].
Option A optimizes for [benefit], but introduces [risk].
Option B trades off [thing] but gives us [advantage].
We recommend [choice] because [evidence]."

Principles

  • Always tie back to user needs or business goals, never personal preference
  • Use data: user research, analytics, competitive analysis, or design principles
  • Acknowledge what you're giving up — it builds trust and shows thorough thinking
  • Present the trade-off as a decision, not a compromise
  • Offer your recommendation with conviction, but stay open to input

Full Example

Real scenario

"We could add inline editing (faster for power users who edit frequently) or keep the modal pattern (clearer separation of read/edit states for new users).

Since 70% of our users are in their first month and support tickets about accidental edits are our #2 issue, we recommend the modal — with a keyboard shortcut (Cmd+E) for power users who want speed.

Trade-off: power users take 2 extra clicks. But we eliminate accidental edits and reduce support load."

Common Mistakes

What to avoid
  • ❌ "I just think it looks better" — no evidence
  • ❌ Presenting only one option — looks like you didn't explore
  • ❌ Making it sound like there's no downside — stakeholders see through this
  • ❌ Using jargon without explaining impact — "better information scent" means nothing to a VP

front: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD)
back:
Core Idea

A framework by Clayton Christensen: customers don't buy products — they "hire" them to get a job done. Focus on the outcome the customer wants, not the features you build.

The Formula

Job statement template

"When I [situation/context],
I want to [motivation/action],
so I can [expected outcome]."

The Classic Example

The drill bit story

People don't buy a 1/4" drill bit — they want a hole in the wall.
They don't want a hole — they want a shelf on the wall.
They don't want a shelf — they want to display their books beautifully.
They don't want to display books — they want a home that feels organized and personal.

Each level deeper reveals a more fundamental job. The deeper you go, the more innovative your solution can be.

How JTBD Shifts Your Thinking

Traditional Thinking → JTBD Thinking ───────────────────── ───────────────────── Demographics Context and intent "Who is our user?" "What are they trying to accomplish?" Feature lists Outcome-driven design Competitor comparison Alternative solutions (your real competition)

Surprising Competitors

Same job, different products

A milkshake's competition isn't other milkshakes. Christensen's research found morning commuters "hired" milkshakes for the job of making a boring drive more interesting while keeping one hand free. Real competitors: bananas, bagels, podcasts.

Using JTBD in Design

Interview users about the last time they did something (not hypotheticals). Ask: What triggered it? What did you try first? What would have to be true for you to switch?

front: Cognitive Load
back:
Definition

The total mental effort required to use an interface. Based on Cognitive Load Theory by John Sweller (1988). Your working memory has limited capacity — design should respect that.

Three Types

  • Intrinsic load — inherent task complexity. Filing taxes is harder than checking weather. You can't change this.
  • Extraneous load — caused by poor design. Confusing layouts, unclear labels, unnecessary steps. Minimize this.
  • Germane load — effort spent learning and forming mental models. Support this with good patterns and feedback.

The Capacity Problem

┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ Working Memory Capacity │ │ ┌──────┬────────────┬────────┐ │ │ │Intrin│ Extraneous │Germane │ │ │ │ sic │ (REDUCE!) │(GOOD!) │ │ │ └──────┴────────────┴────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ If extraneous fills the space, no room left for learning!

Tactics to Reduce Extraneous Load

  • Hick's Law — more choices = longer decisions. Reduce options to essentials.
  • Chunking — group information into digestible groups of 3-5 items
  • Progressive disclosure — show details on demand, not all at once
  • Familiar patterns — use conventions users already know (e.g., cart icon = shopping)
  • Visual hierarchy — size, color, and spacing guide attention naturally

Real-World Example

Checkout form

A 15-field checkout form on one page = high extraneous load. Breaking it into 3 steps (Shipping → Payment → Review) with a progress bar reduces perceived complexity, even though the total work is the same. Users complete it more often because each step feels manageable.

front: OKRs (Objectives and Key Results)
back:
What It Is

A goal-setting framework popularized by Andy Grove at Intel and later adopted by Google. It connects aspirational goals to measurable outcomes.

Structure

The formula

Objective = qualitative, inspirational, time-bound
"Where do we want to go?"

Key Results = 2-5 measurable outcomes per objective
"How will we know we got there?"

Design Team Example

Q2 OKR

Objective: Make onboarding delightful and effortless

  • KR1: Reduce time-to-first-value from 5 min to 2 min
  • KR2: Increase setup completion rate from 60% to 85%
  • KR3: Decrease onboarding-related support tickets by 40%
  • KR4: Achieve NPS score of 50+ for new user first-week experience

OKR vs. KPI

OKR KPI ─── ─── Aspirations + outcomes Ongoing health metrics Set quarterly, change often Stable over time "Where are we going?" "How are we doing?" 70% completion = success 100% = target Example: Example: "Reduce churn by 20%" (OKR) "Monthly churn rate" (KPI)

Tips for Designers

  • Connect your design work to KRs to demonstrate impact in reviews
  • Set quarterly, review weekly at standups
  • Aim for 70% achievement — 100% means your goals were too easy
  • Write KRs as outcomes, not tasks. Not "Ship redesign" but "Improve task completion rate to 90%"

front: Progressive Disclosure
back:
Definition

A design pattern that defers advanced or secondary features to later interactions, keeping the primary interface simple and focused.

Core principle: Show only what's needed at each step. Complexity on demand.

The Spectrum

Simple ←──────────────────────→ Complex Show basics Reveal on Full power for everyone interaction for experts │ │ │ Main screen Expand/hover/ Settings/ click to see advanced mode more options

Examples in the Wild

Common patterns
  • "Show more options" expandable link — Gmail's compose window
  • Accordion menus — FAQ pages that reveal answers on tap
  • Wizard flows — multi-step forms that break complexity into stages
  • Tooltips on hover — extra context without cluttering the UI
  • "Advanced settings" — hidden by default, available when needed
  • Truncated text with "Read more" — social media posts

Benefits

  • Reduces cognitive load for beginners
  • Still supports power users who need depth
  • Makes interfaces feel less overwhelming on first encounter
  • Improves scannability — less noise, faster decisions

When NOT to Use

Anti-patterns

Don't hide information that's critical to the user's immediate decision. Example: hiding the price behind a "Show details" click on an e-commerce page is frustrating, not helpful. The user needs that info to decide.

Also don't overdo it — too many clicks to reach basic info feels like the UI is hiding things from you.

front: How to present a design critique professionally
back:
The Framework

Use "I like / I wish / What if" — a structured approach from Stanford d.school that keeps critique constructive and specific.

Template
  • I like [specific positive element] because [why it works for users]
  • I wish [specific concern] because [user impact]
  • What if [constructive alternative that addresses the concern]

Good vs. Bad Critique

Comparison

❌ "I don't like the placement."
Vague, subjective, unhelpful.

✅ "Users might struggle to find this CTA because it breaks the expected F-pattern scan. What if we moved it above the fold, closer to the value proposition?"
Specific, user-centered, offers an alternative.

Ground Rules for Great Critiques

  • Ground feedback in user needs and design principles, not personal taste
  • Be specific — point to the exact element, not "the layout"
  • Critique the work, not the designer — "this button" not "your button"
  • Offer alternatives, not just problems
  • Acknowledge constraints the designer worked within
  • Ask questions: "What led you to this approach?" before suggesting changes

Receiving Critique

When it's your design
  • Listen first, don't defend immediately
  • Take notes — you'll forget details later
  • Ask clarifying questions: "Can you say more about what feels off?"
  • Remember: they're critiquing the design, not you
  • Say "thank you" — it takes effort to give good feedback

front: Design System
back:
Definition

A collection of reusable components, guided by clear standards and documentation, that can be assembled to build applications consistently and efficiently.

What's Inside

  • UI components — buttons, inputs, modals, cards, navigation
  • Design tokens — colors, spacing, typography, border-radius, shadows
  • Patterns — common layouts, form patterns, data display conventions
  • Documentation — when and how to use each component
  • Usage guidelines — dos, don'ts, accessibility requirements

Anatomy of a Design System

┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Design Principles │ │ (values that guide decisions) │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ Design Tokens (atoms) │ │ colors, spacing, type, shadows │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ Components (molecules) │ │ buttons, inputs, cards, modals │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ Patterns (organisms) │ │ forms, navigation, data tables │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ Page Templates │ │ dashboard, settings, onboarding │ └─────────────────────────────────┘

Well-Known Design Systems

Industry examples
  • Material Design — Google (most widely adopted)
  • Polaris — Shopify (e-commerce focused)
  • Carbon — IBM (enterprise/data-heavy)
  • Lightning — Salesforce (CRM patterns)
  • Primer — GitHub (developer tools)
  • Spectrum — Adobe (creative tools)

Why It Matters

Consistency — users learn once, use everywhere. Speed — teams ship faster when they're not reinventing buttons. Scalability — as the product grows, the system keeps it coherent. Quality — accessibility and best practices are baked in once.

front: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a stakeholder's decision.
back:
How to Structure

Use the STAR method — it keeps your answer focused and impactful:

  • Situation — brief context (2 sentences max)
  • Task — your specific role and responsibility
  • Action — what you did (not the team)
  • Result — outcome with measurable impact

Example Answer

Strong STAR response

S: A PM wanted to skip user testing on a checkout redesign to meet a Q3 deadline. The redesign changed the entire payment flow.

T: As lead designer, I needed to protect quality without blocking the team's momentum.

A: I proposed a compromise — a lightweight 3-person guerrilla test that could run in one day instead of a full 2-week study. I recruited participants from our support channel, set up tasks in the prototype, and ran all sessions back-to-back in an afternoon.

R: We found a critical flow issue: users couldn't tell their card was saved vs. being charged. That would have generated support tickets and possible chargebacks. We fixed it in 2 hours, and shipped only 2 days later than original timeline — with a flow that had a 94% completion rate vs. the old 71%.

Key Principles

  • Never badmouth the stakeholder — show respect for their perspective
  • Show you collaborated, not combated — you found a middle path
  • Emphasize the outcome, not the conflict itself
  • Show initiative — you proposed a solution, not just pushback
  • Keep it under 2 minutes — practice timing yourself

What They're Really Asking

Hidden evaluation criteria

This question tests: Can you disagree productively? Can you advocate for users while maintaining relationships? Do you default to data over ego? The answer should leave the interviewer thinking "I'd want this person on my team."

front: Walk me through your design process for a recent project.
back:
The 5-Phase Structure

Keep your walkthrough to 3-4 minutes. Interviewers want decision-making, not a feature tour.

  1. Discovery — what you learned about users and constraints
  2. Define — how you framed the problem (the insight)
  3. Ideation — options explored and why you chose one
  4. Execution — your final solution with design rationale
  5. Impact — measurable outcomes and what you learned

What to Emphasize at Each Stage

Phase Show this Not this ───── ───────── ──────── Discovery Research methods "I talked to users" Define The insight/reframe Just the brief Ideation Why you chose X List of ideas Execution Rationale for Pixel walkthrough design decisions Impact Metrics + learnings "It shipped"

Pro Tips

What makes answers stand out
  • Show decision-making, not just deliverables — "I chose this because..."
  • Interviewers want to hear WHY, not just WHAT
  • Include at least one constraint you navigated (time, tech, politics)
  • Mention something you killed or changed based on feedback
  • End with what you'd do differently — shows growth mindset
  • Tailor the project to the role you're interviewing for

Common Mistakes

Avoid these
  • ❌ Spending 10 minutes on context before showing design work
  • ❌ Skipping the problem and jumping to the solution
  • ❌ Showing every iteration instead of the key turning points
  • ❌ Taking full credit when it was a team effort (interviewers always ask follow-ups)

front: If you could redesign any everyday object, what would it be and why?
back:
What This Tests

Design thinking on the spot. There's no right answer — they're evaluating your thought process, curiosity, and communication.

How to Structure Your Answer

  1. Pick something you genuinely find frustrating — authenticity shows
  2. Identify the specific pain point — not just "it's bad" but exactly what fails
  3. Who's affected and in what context does the problem occur
  4. Your redesign concept — sketch the idea verbally with enough detail
  5. Trade-offs you'd consider — cost, feasibility, edge cases

Example Answer

Strong response

"I'd redesign elevator panels. The problem: once you press your floor, there's no way to know if someone else has already requested a stop before yours. You stand there with zero information about your estimated wait.

My redesign: a small e-ink display on each panel showing all requested stops in order, with your floor highlighted and an estimated time. Think of it like a subway countdown clock.

Trade-offs: screen cost per elevator (~$50 per panel), but it reduces rider anxiety and could help building management understand traffic patterns. The ROI comes from reduced "people holding doors" incidents and better perceived service quality."

What Makes Answers Stand Out

Evaluation criteria
  • Specificity — "the microwave timer interface" beats "microwaves"
  • User empathy — who struggles and why
  • Feasibility awareness — acknowledging constraints
  • Clear articulation — can you paint a picture with words?

front: Describe a project that failed. What did you learn?
back:
Why They Ask This

Choose a real failure — interviewers see through fake or trivial answers. They want to see self-awareness, accountability, and growth.

Structure

  1. What happened and your specific role in it
  2. What went wrong — be honest and specific about root causes
  3. What you learned and how it changed your behavior
  4. What you'd do differently with what you know now

Example Answer

Strong response

"I designed a collaboration feature for a project management tool based entirely on stakeholder requests — the VP wanted it, so we built it. I created a beautiful Slack-like chat panel inside the app.

After launch, adoption was 12%. Users already had Slack. They didn't want another place to chat. I had designed a solution to a problem that didn't exist for users — only for internal stakeholders who wanted 'engagement metrics.'

What I learned: always validate the problem with users before designing the solution. A stakeholder request is a hypothesis, not a requirement. Now I insist on at least 5 user interviews or a survey before committing design resources to a new feature. Even 30-minute conversations with 3 users would have saved us 6 weeks."

What Makes a Great Answer

  • Ownership — don't blame the PM, the timeline, or engineering
  • Specific learning — not generic "I learned to communicate better"
  • Behavioral change — show what you do now as a direct result
  • Proportional failure — pick something meaningful, not "I used the wrong shade of blue"

Red Flags to Avoid

Don't do this
  • ❌ "I've never really had a project fail" — unbelievable and arrogant
  • ❌ Blaming everyone else — shows no self-awareness
  • ❌ Picking a trivial example — suggests you haven't taken real risks
  • ❌ No concrete behavioral change — learning without action isn't learning

front: Your team has conflicting user research findings. How do you proceed?
back:
Show Analytical Thinking

Conflicting data is normal — how you handle it reveals your maturity as a designer.

Step-by-Step Approach

  1. Examine methodology — Were the studies asking different questions? Different sample sizes? Different user segments? Different contexts?
  2. Look for synthesis — Maybe both findings are true for different user groups. Segment the data.
  3. Check for bias — Leading questions? Confirmation bias in analysis? Selection bias in recruitment?
  4. Propose resolution — Additional research to fill gaps, or A/B testing competing hypotheses
  5. Prioritize — When you must decide, go with the most robust sample and quantitative backing

Triangulation Framework

Cross-reference 3 data sources
Qualitative Quantitative (interviews) (analytics) ╲ ╱ ╲ SIGNAL? ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ Behavioral (support tickets, session recordings)

When 2+ sources agree, you have a stronger signal. Conflicting data usually means you're looking at different user segments with different needs — which is itself a valuable insight.

Real Example

How this plays out

"Survey says users want more features. Usability tests show users are overwhelmed by the interface." These aren't contradictory — users want capability but need better progressive disclosure. The answer isn't fewer features or more features — it's better organization of existing ones.

front: You have 30 minutes to improve our product's sign-up flow. Go.
back:
Don't Panic

This is about structured thinking under pressure, not a perfect solution. They're testing your process, not your mockups.

Your 30-Minute Plan

0-5 min Ask clarifying questions 5-10 min Audit the current flow 10-20 min Identify top friction + sketch fixes 20-25 min Explain reasoning + expected impact 25-30 min What you'd test + measure

Step 1: Clarifying Questions

Ask these first
  • What's the current conversion rate? Where's the biggest drop-off?
  • Mobile or desktop? (Or both — which matters more?)
  • What data do we collect during sign-up? Is all of it necessary at this stage?
  • Are there any technical or business constraints I should know about?

Step 2: Common Friction Points

  • Too many fields — every field reduces conversion ~7%
  • Password requirements shown only after submission → show inline
  • No social sign-up — Google/Apple login reduces friction dramatically
  • No progress indication on multi-step flows
  • Email verification blocking the experience before showing value

Strong Closing

How to wrap up

"My hypothesis is that [specific friction point] causes the biggest drop-off. I'd validate by setting up a [specific test]. The metric I'd watch is [specific metric]. If I had more time, I'd also explore [stretch idea]."

Saying "I'd want to look at the data first" shows maturity. It's better than guessing.

front: How do you handle tight deadlines with high design expectations?
back:
Show Pragmatism

The answer isn't "I work harder" — it's "I prioritize smarter."

The Tiered Approach

Priority What When ──────── ──── ──── Must-have Core user need Ship now Should-have Better experience Next sprint Nice-to-have Polish & delight Backlog ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Ship the must-haves │ │ POLISHED. Iterate the rest.│ └─────────────────────────────┘

Tactical Approaches

  1. Prioritize ruthlessly — What's the minimum lovable experience? Not MVP, but MLE.
  2. Communicate trade-offs transparently — make the decision visible, not silent
  3. Lean on your design system — existing patterns ship faster than custom work
  4. Time-box exploration — "I'll spend 2 hours exploring, then commit to one direction"
  5. Negotiate scope, not quality — ship fewer features well, never ship many features poorly

How to Frame the Conversation

Script for stakeholders

"We can ship A and B by Friday, or A, B, and C by next Wednesday. Here's what users lose if we cut C: [specific impact]. Here's what we gain with the extra time: [specific benefit]. Which matters more right now?"

This makes the trade-off their decision, not your excuse.

What Interviewers Want to Hear

Key signals
  • You can distinguish between essential and nice-to-have
  • You communicate proactively, not at the last minute
  • You have a system, not just willpower
  • You protect quality of what ships, even if scope shrinks

front: Design a product for aliens who communicate through colors.
back:
Why They Ask This

Absurd prompts test creativity under ambiguity. They want to see if you can apply design thinking to any problem, even one you've never encountered.

Structured Approach

  1. Don't laugh it off — treat it with genuine curiosity
  2. Ask constraints as questions (shows you think before designing):
    • How many colors can they perceive? (Our visible spectrum? More?)
    • Is color communication sequential or simultaneous?
    • Is it real-time or asynchronous?
    • Do colors have fixed meanings or context-dependent ones?
  3. Propose a grounded solution using universal design principles
  4. Draw parallels to real challenges you can speak to

Example Answer

Thought process

"I'd start with a 'Rosetta Stone' onboarding — teach the system 10 base colors and their meanings through visual association. Think of it like how Duolingo teaches vocabulary through pictures, not translations.

The UI would be a chromatic keyboard — a color wheel where you compose messages by combining and sequencing colors. Received messages would render as animated color patterns with an optional 'translation layer' for mixed-species communication."

Real-World Parallels

This isn't as wild as it sounds
  • Designing for non-verbal users — AAC devices use symbols instead of words
  • Cross-cultural color meaning — red = luck (China) vs. danger (West)
  • Emoji as language — visual communication without grammar rules
  • Music as communication — conveying emotion without words

Show you can think beyond the obvious and apply design principles to unfamiliar territory. The creativity is in the process, not the answer.

front: An engineer says your design is technically impossible. What do you do?
back:
Mindset

This is about collaboration, not conflict. The best designers and engineers build solutions together.

Step-by-Step Response

  1. Listen fully — understand the technical constraint before responding. Don't get defensive.
  2. Ask "what IS possible?" — find the boundary between impossible and expensive
  3. Separate intent from implementation — "The UX goal is [X]. What's the closest we can get technically?"
  4. Collaborate on alternatives that preserve the user experience intent
  5. Evaluate the trade-off — is this detail worth the engineering investment?
  6. Document the decision — so future teams understand why

The Magic Question

Ask this every time

"If we had unlimited time, could we do this? What makes it hard?"

This separates truly impossible from expensive/complex. The answer is usually: "We could, but it would take 3 sprints instead of 1." Now it's a prioritization decision, not a technical limitation. That's a conversation you can have with product.

Spectrum of Responses

"Impossible" → What they often mean: ───────────────────────────────────── "Can't do it" → "Not with our current stack" "Too complex" → "It would take 3x longer" "Edge cases" → "Works for 90%, breaks for 10%" "Performance" → "Possible but slow" "Not in the API" → "We'd need backend changes"

What Interviewers Want to See

Evaluation criteria
  • You respect engineering expertise and don't dismiss constraints
  • You're flexible on implementation but firm on user impact
  • You problem-solve collaboratively, not dictate solutions
  • You understand that shipping imperfect > not shipping perfect

front: Tell me about a time you used data to influence a design decision.
back:
Structure with STAR

Bridge qualitative and quantitative — the strongest answers show you can use both.

Example Answer

Full STAR response

S: Our e-commerce checkout had a 40% abandonment rate at the address step — significantly higher than industry benchmark (25%).

T: I needed to find why users were leaving and design a fix within 2 sprints.

A: I started with the quantitative data — analytics showed users were rage-clicking the address fields and 30% attempted to go back. Then I ran 5 usability sessions to understand why. Users were confused by auto-fill behavior: the browser auto-filled their old address, but our validation rejected the format, showing cryptic error messages. I redesigned the form with clearer field states, real-time inline validation, and an explicit "Use a different address" link.

R: Abandonment at the address step dropped from 40% to 18%. Support tickets about checkout issues fell by 35%. The total checkout completion rate improved by 12 percentage points.

The Data → Design Connection

Quantitative (WHERE) Qualitative (WHY) ──────────────────── ──────────────── Analytics, heatmaps Interviews, testing Funnel drop-off rates Session recordings A/B test results Support ticket themes │ │ └──────┬───────────────┘ │ Design Decision │ Measurable Outcome

Key Principles

  • Use quantitative data to find where the problem is
  • Use qualitative data to find why it's happening
  • Always close the loop: Insight → Action → Measurable Outcome
  • Even small datasets are valuable — 5 usability sessions catch 85% of issues

front: What's overrated in design right now?
back:
What This Tests

Your ability to have a genuine, defensible point of view. Not a hot take for shock value — a thoughtful perspective backed by reasoning.

How to Answer

  • Pick something you can defend thoughtfully with real experience
  • Avoid attacking specific people or companies
  • Back it up with reasoning and examples
  • Stay respectful of different approaches — "overrated" ≠ "worthless"
  • Acknowledge the kernel of value in the thing you're critiquing

Example Answer

Strong response

"I think pixel-perfect Dribbble aesthetics are overrated in the industry right now. Beautiful mockups that weren't validated with users often ship features nobody needs. I've seen teams spend weeks polishing visual details on flows that users never even reach.

I'd rather see a rough prototype that solved a real problem than a gorgeous screen that sits in a portfolio. That said, visual quality does matter — it builds trust. The issue is when aesthetics become the goal instead of a means to better usability."

Other Strong Angles

Topics you could pick
  • "Dark mode everything" — sometimes light mode is better for readability and data-heavy interfaces
  • "Design thinking workshops" — they've become performative theater in some orgs, where sticky notes replace actual user contact
  • "AI-generated UI" — produces generic-looking interfaces that lack the intentionality of human design decisions
  • "Micro-interactions on everything" — delightful once, annoying after the 100th time when you're trying to get work done

What Makes the Answer Work

The interviewer is checking: Do you think independently? Can you articulate a position without being dismissive? Do you distinguish between trend-following and principled design?

front: How would you onboard yourself in the first 90 days at a new company?
back:
The 30-60-90 Framework

Shows you're structured, proactive, and balance learning with contributing.

Days 1-30: Listen & Learn

Focus: understand before you change anything
  • Meet every stakeholder — PM, eng leads, support, sales, marketing
  • Review existing research, past design decisions, and the design system
  • Shadow customer support calls and watch session recordings
  • Map the product ecosystem — how does everything connect?
  • Understand the team's current process, rituals, and pain points
  • Ask: "What's been tried before and didn't work?" — avoids repeating mistakes

Days 31-60: Contribute

Focus: earn trust through small, visible wins
  • Pick up a well-scoped project — ideally something with clear impact
  • Build relationships with engineering counterparts through pairing
  • Identify quick wins — small improvements that show taste and initiative
  • Start contributing to design critiques with the "I like / I wish / What if" framework
  • Share early observations — "fresh eyes" insights are most valuable now

Days 61-90: Lead

Focus: own outcomes, not just outputs
  • Own a feature or initiative end-to-end
  • Propose process improvements based on what you've observed
  • Share your "fresh eyes" report — what the company can't see because they're too close
  • Begin shaping the roadmap with PM — transition from executor to thought partner

Key Signal

The interviewer wants to see you balance humility ("I'll learn the context first") with initiative ("I won't just observe for 3 months"). Both extremes are red flags.

front: Variable: let vs. const
back:
The Basics

const declares a variable that can't be reassigned. let declares one that can. There's also var (old-style) — avoid it.

Rule of Thumb

Use const by default. Only use let when you know the value needs to change.

Side by Side

const — value won't change

const name = 'Selina';
const API_URL = 'https://api.example.com';
const colors = ['red', 'blue'];

let — value will change

let count = 0;
count++; // now 1

let isLoggedIn = false;
isLoggedIn = true; // after auth

The Biggest Gotcha

const doesn't mean immutable!

const user = { name: 'Selina' };
user.name = 'Alex'; ← totally fine!
user = { name: 'Alex' }; ← ERROR!

With objects and arrays, const locks the reference (which box), not the contents (what's in the box). You can change properties, add items, etc. You just can't point the variable at a completely different object.

When to Use Which

const → 95% of the time function params, imports, config, DOM references let → 5% of the time counters, accumulators, values that update in loops var → 0% of the time legacy code only

front: What is the DOM?
back:
Definition

Document Object Model — the browser's live representation of your HTML as a tree of JavaScript objects that you can read, change, and manipulate.

The Tree Structure

document │ <html> ╱ ╲ <head> <body> │ │ <title> <div id="app"> ╱ ╲ <h1> <p> "Hello" "Welcome"

Every HTML element becomes a node in this tree. JavaScript can access and modify any node.

Key Operations

Finding elements

document.getElementById('title')
document.querySelector('.card')
document.querySelectorAll('.card-tag')

Changing elements

el.textContent = 'New text';
el.style.color = 'red';
el.classList.add('active');
el.classList.toggle('hidden');
el.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Close');

Creating elements

const div = document.createElement('div');
div.className = 'card';
div.innerHTML = '<h3>Title</h3>';
parent.appendChild(div);

Why It Matters

When you "manipulate the DOM," you're changing what the user sees in real time without reloading the page. Every interactive website — from clicking a button to infinite scroll — works by reading and writing to the DOM.

Performance Note

DOM operations are expensive. Changing the DOM triggers the browser to recalculate layout and repaint. That's why frameworks like React use a "virtual DOM" — they batch changes and apply them efficiently.

front: Functions: How to define reusable code
back:
What Is a Function?

A named block of code you can call repeatedly. Functions are the building blocks of any program — they let you organize, reuse, and test code.

Two Common Styles

Function declaration

function greet(name) {
  return 'Hello ' + name;
}
greet('Selina'); // 'Hello Selina'

Arrow function (modern)

const greet = (name) => 'Hello ' + name;

// Multi-line version:
const greet = (name) => {
  const message = 'Hello ' + name;
  return message;
};

Anatomy

function greet(name) { ← name + parameters return 'Hello ' + name; ← body (logic) } ← return (output) greet('Selina') ← calling the function └─ argument ← value passed in

Key Concepts

  • Parameters — placeholders the function accepts (defined)
  • Arguments — actual values you pass when calling (used)
  • Return value — the output. Without return, the function returns undefined
  • Side effects — things a function does besides returning (logging, DOM changes, API calls)

When to Create One

Rule of thumb

If you see repeated logic, extract it into a function. If you're copy-pasting code, it should probably be a function. If a chunk of code does one clear thing, naming it as a function makes your code self-documenting.

front: Array methods: map, filter, find
back:
Why These Matter

Three essential array methods that replace manual for-loops with cleaner, more readable code. You'll use these constantly.

map — Transform Every Item

Creates a new array by transforming each element

[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2)
→ [2, 4, 6]

users.map(u => u.name)
→ ['Selina', 'Alex', 'Jordan']

Use when: You want to transform data without changing the original.

filter — Keep Items That Pass a Test

Returns only elements where the callback returns true

[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].filter(x => x > 3)
→ [4, 5]

cards.filter(c => c.category === 'ux')
→ only UX cards

Use when: You want a subset of data based on a condition.

find — Return First Match

Returns the first element that passes, or undefined

[1, 2, 3].find(x => x > 1)
→ 2 (just the first match, not all)

users.find(u => u.id === 'abc')
→ that specific user object

Use when: You want exactly one item by ID or condition.

Chaining

data .filter(item => item.active) // keep active .map(item => item.name) // get names .sort() // alphabetize → ['Alex', 'Jordan', 'Selina']

Key: These return new arrays/values — they never modify the original. This is called immutability and prevents bugs.

front: CSS Flexbox: Aligning things in a row or column
back:
What It Is

Flexbox is a CSS layout model for arranging items in one direction — either a row or a column. Apply display: flex to a container to control its children.

The Two Axes

flex-direction: row (default) ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ │ │ │ A │ │ B │ │ C │ │ → main axis │ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ │ (justify-content) └──────────────────────────────────┘ ↕ cross axis (align-items) flex-direction: column ┌──────────┐ │ ┌────┐ │ │ │ A │ │ → cross axis (align-items) │ └────┘ │ │ ┌────┐ │ │ │ B │ │ ↓ main axis │ └────┘ │ (justify-content) │ ┌────┐ │ │ │ C │ │ │ └────┘ │ └──────────┘

Key Properties

  • flex-direction — row (default) or column
  • justify-content — alignment along main axis: center, space-between, space-around
  • align-items — alignment along cross axis: center, stretch, flex-start
  • gap — spacing between items (modern, clean)
  • flex-wrap — wrap allows items to flow to next line

The Most Useful Pattern

Center anything on screen

display: flex;
justify-content: center;
align-items: center;
min-height: 100vh;

This centers a child element both horizontally and vertically. You'll use this pattern constantly — login screens, modals, empty states, loading spinners.

Common Layouts

Navigation bar

display: flex;
justify-content: space-between;
align-items: center;

Logo on left, menu on right — done.

front: Event Listeners: Making things interactive
back:
What They Do

Event listeners let your code respond to user actions. Every interactive element on the web uses them — buttons, forms, scroll effects, keyboard shortcuts.

Syntax

element.addEventListener('eventType', callbackFunction)

Common Events

Mouse & touch
  • 'click' — tap or click (works on both mobile and desktop)
  • 'dblclick' — double click
  • 'mouseenter' / 'mouseleave' — hover in/out
  • 'touchstart' / 'touchend' — mobile touch
Form & input
  • 'input' — fires on every keystroke in a field
  • 'change' — fires when field loses focus after changing
  • 'submit' — form submission (use e.preventDefault()!)
  • 'focus' / 'blur' — element gains/loses focus
Keyboard & other
  • 'keydown' / 'keyup' — keyboard press/release
  • 'scroll' — page or element scroll
  • 'resize' — window size changes

Full Example

Button click handler

const btn = document.querySelector('.save-btn');
btn.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  e.preventDefault();
  saveData();
  btn.textContent = 'Saved!';
});

Tips

  • Always use addEventListener over inline onclick — it's cleaner and supports multiple listeners
  • Use e.preventDefault() to stop default browser behavior (form submit, link navigation)
  • Use e.stopPropagation() to prevent the event from bubbling up to parent elements

front: JSON: How data is stored and transferred
back:
What It Is

JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight text format for storing and transferring structured data. It's the lingua franca of the web.

What It Looks Like

A JSON object

{
  "name": "Selina",
  "role": "designer",
  "skills": ["UX", "UI", "research"],
  "experience": {
    "years": 3,
    "current": true
  }
}

Rules

  • Keys must be double-quoted strings (not single quotes!)
  • Values can be: strings, numbers, booleans, null, arrays, objects
  • No trailing commas allowed
  • No comments allowed (unlike regular JS)
  • No functions or undefined

In JavaScript

Converting between JS objects and JSON

// Object → JSON string
const str = JSON.stringify(myObj);

// JSON string → Object
const obj = JSON.parse(str);

Where You'll See It

API responses ←→ Server sends JSON to browser Config files ←→ package.json, tsconfig.json localStorage ←→ Must stringify before saving Data files ←→ Your cards.json! Fetch requests ←→ Send JSON in POST body

Common Mistake

Watch out for this

localStorage.setItem('data', myObj); ← WRONG! Saves "[object Object]"
localStorage.setItem('data', JSON.stringify(myObj)); ← Correct

front: localStorage: Saving data in the browser
back:
What It Is

A browser API for persisting key-value data across page reloads and browser sessions. Data stays until the user clears it or your code removes it.

Basic Usage

The three core methods

// Save
localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark');

// Read
localStorage.getItem('theme'); // 'dark'

// Remove
localStorage.removeItem('theme');

// Clear everything
localStorage.clear();

The String Problem

localStorage only stores strings. Numbers, booleans, objects, arrays — all become strings.

Saving complex data

// Save an object
const data = { score: 42, level: 3 };
localStorage.setItem('game', JSON.stringify(data));

// Load it back
const loaded = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('game'));
// { score: 42, level: 3 }

Safe Reading Pattern

Handle missing data gracefully

function getStorage(key) {
  try {
    return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(key)) || [];
  } catch {
    return [];
  }
}

This handles: key doesn't exist (returns []), corrupted data (returns []), valid data (returns parsed).

Limits & Alternatives

localStorage ~5MB per origin sessionStorage ~5MB, cleared when tab closes IndexedDB Much larger, async, complex Cookies ~4KB, sent with every request Server database Unlimited, requires backend

Use localStorage for: user preferences, saved state, small datasets. Never for: passwords, tokens, or sensitive data.

front: async/await: Handling things that take time
back:
The Problem

Some operations take time — fetching data from a server, reading files, waiting for user input. JavaScript doesn't pause and wait by default — it keeps running. async/await lets you write asynchronous code that reads like synchronous code.

The Pattern

Basic async/await

async function loadData() {
  const response = await fetch('data.json');
  const data = await response.json();
  return data;
}

How It Works

Without await: With await: ────────────── ─────────── fetch(url) ← starts await fetch(url) // keeps going ↓ pauses here // data not ready yet! // data IS ready console.log(data) ← 💥 console.log(data) ← ✅

Key Rules

  • Mark the function as async — this is required to use await inside it
  • Use await before any Promise-returning operation (fetch, setTimeout wrapper, etc.)
  • Without await, your code moves on before data arrives — leading to undefined errors
  • async functions always return a Promise

Error Handling

Always wrap in try/catch

async function loadUser() {
  try {
    const res = await fetch('/api/user');
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Failed');
    return await res.json();
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Load failed:', error);
    showErrorMessage();
  }
}

Common Mistake

Forgetting await

const data = fetch('data.json');
console.log(data); // Promise {<pending>} 😱

const data = await fetch('data.json');
console.log(data); // Response object ✅

front: CSS Selectors: Targeting elements to style
back:
What They Do

Selectors tell CSS which HTML elements to style. Getting selectors right is the foundation of all CSS work.

Basic Selectors

The essentials
  • div — by tag name (all divs)
  • .card — by class (any element with class="card")
  • #header — by ID (unique element)
  • div.card — div that has class card
  • * — everything (universal selector)

Combinators

Relationship-based selection
  • .card .title — descendant (any depth inside .card)
  • .card > .title — direct child only (one level deep)
  • .card + .card — adjacent sibling (the next .card)
  • .card ~ .card — general sibling (all following .cards)

Pseudo-classes

State-based styling
  • :hover — mouse over (desktop)
  • :focus — keyboard/click focus
  • :active — being pressed
  • :first-child / :last-child — position in parent
  • :nth-child(odd) — every other element
  • :not(.disabled) — exclusion

Specificity

Specificity ranking (highest → lowest): inline style="" → 1,0,0,0 #id → 0,1,0,0 .class → 0,0,1,0 tag → 0,0,0,1 .card .title → 0,0,2,0 (2 classes) #main .card → 0,1,1,0 (1 id + 1 class)

Best Practices

  • Mostly use classes — they're reusable and have the right specificity balance
  • Avoid IDs for styling (too specific, hard to override)
  • Avoid !important — it's a sign of a specificity problem
  • Keep selectors shallow — .card-title beats .feed .card-stack .card .card-inner .title

front: Reading error messages in the browser console
back:
How to Open DevTools

Mac: Cmd + Option + I → Console tab
Windows: F12 or Ctrl + Shift + I → Console tab

Anatomy of an Error

Example error

TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined
  at renderCard (cards.js:42)
  at buildQueue (cards.js:108)
  at start (app.js:22)

How to Read It

TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined │ │ │ │ │ └─ The value was │ │ undefined/null │ └─ What you tried to do └─ Error category at renderCard (cards.js:42) ← WHERE it happened at buildQueue (cards.js:108) ← WHO called it at start (app.js:22) ← The chain of calls

Most Common Errors

  • Cannot read property of undefined — you're accessing a property on something that doesn't exist. Trace back to where the variable was set.
  • X is not a function — you're calling something that isn't a function (maybe it's undefined, or you misspelled it)
  • X is not defined — the variable doesn't exist in the current scope (typo? forgot to declare?)
  • SyntaxError — your code has a typo: missing bracket, extra comma, unclosed string
  • NetworkError — a fetch/API call failed (wrong URL, server down, CORS issue)

Debugging Steps

When you hit an error
  1. Read the error message — it usually tells you exactly what's wrong
  2. Click the file:line link — DevTools takes you right to the problem
  3. Add console.log() before the error line to inspect values
  4. Check if data exists — most errors are from missing/null data
  5. Google the exact error message — someone has hit it before

front: Template Literals: Building dynamic strings
back:
What They Are

Use backticks ` instead of quotes to embed JavaScript expressions directly inside strings. Modern, clean, and powerful.

Before vs. After

Old way (concatenation)

'Hello ' + name + ', you have ' + count + ' items'
Hard to read, easy to miss spaces and quotes.

New way (template literal)

`Hello ${name}, you have ${count} items`
Clean, readable, and ${} can contain any expression.

Superpowers

  • Multi-line strings — no more \n or string concatenation across lines
  • Expressions inside ${} — variables, math, function calls, ternaries
  • Building HTML in JS — the most common real-world use case

Expression Examples

You can put anything in ${}

`Total: $${price * quantity}` → "Total: $150"
`Status: ${isActive ? 'Active' : 'Inactive'}`
`Name: ${user.name.toUpperCase()}`
`Items: ${items.length}`

Building HTML (Most Common Use)

Real-world pattern

el.innerHTML = `
  <div class="card ${isFlipped ? 'flipped' : ''}">
    <h3>${card.title}</h3>
    <p>${card.description}</p>
    <span class="tag">${card.category}</span>
  </div>
`;

This is exactly how your Learning Hub renders cards! Template literals make it easy to build dynamic HTML without a framework.

front: Concessive Clause: 'Although / Even though'
back:
What It Does

Acknowledges a point before presenting a contrasting conclusion. It signals "I know this is true, BUT here's what matters more."

Structure

Although [concession], [main point].
[Main point], even though [concession].

Professional Examples

In design contexts
  • "Although the initial research was inconclusive, the follow-up study confirmed our hypothesis."
  • "Even though the deadline was tight, we delivered a polished experience."
  • "While the data is limited, the trend is clear enough to act on."
  • "Despite the technical constraints, we found a solution that preserved the core UX."

Strategic Word Order

Order changes the emphasis

Soften bad news (concession first):
"Although we missed the deadline, the quality was significantly higher than the original scope."
Lands on the positive.

Emphasize achievement despite odds (concession last):
"We shipped on time, even though the scope expanded twice and we lost a team member mid-sprint."
Lands on the challenge overcome.

Variation & Nuance

Word Formality Use when ──── ───────── ──────── Although Formal Written, presentations Even though Neutral Speaking, emphasis While Neutral Softer contrast Despite Formal + noun ("despite the delay") Regardless Formal Dismissing the concern Notwithstanding Very formal Legal, executive comms

Common Mistake

Don't use "but" after a concessive clause — the contrast is already built in.
❌ "Although it was late, but we finished."
✅ "Although it was late, we finished."

front: Transition: Moving to the next point in a presentation
back:
Why Transitions Matter

Strong transitions signal structure and keep your audience oriented. Without them, your presentation feels like a list of disconnected slides.

Connecting Transitions

Bridge what you just said to what's next
  • "Building on that..."
  • "This brings us to..."
  • "With that context in mind..."
  • "Another dimension of this is..."
  • "That finding leads us to an important question..."

Clean Break Transitions

When switching topics entirely
  • "Now, shifting to..."
  • "Let's zoom out and look at..."
  • "On a related note..."
  • "Turning to the data side of this..."
  • "Let me take a step back and address..."

What to Avoid

❌ Weak ✅ Strong ───────── ────────── "So, um, next..." "Building on that insight..." "Moving on..." "This brings us to..." "Okay so..." "With that context in mind..." "Next slide..." "Now let's look at how this plays out in practice..."

The Bridge Technique

Best practice

The strongest transitions summarize the last point and preview the next in one sentence:

"So we've seen that users struggle with onboarding — now let's look at three specific moments where we can reduce friction."

This gives your audience a mental map: where they've been, where they're going.

front: Leverage (verb) — professional usage
back:
Definition

To use something to maximum advantage. Originally a physics term (using a lever to amplify force), now used in business to mean using an existing resource strategically.

Good Usage

When there's genuine strategic value
  • "We can leverage the existing user research to inform this new feature — no need to start from scratch."
  • "Let's leverage the design system components rather than building custom UI."
  • "This partnership lets us leverage their distribution network."

Overuse Warning

When it becomes jargon

❌ "Let's leverage this meeting to leverage our synergies and leverage the Q3 roadmap."
✅ "Let's use this meeting to align on Q3 priorities."

Rule of thumb: If you can replace "leverage" with "use" and the sentence sounds the same, just say "use." Reserve "leverage" for when there's a genuine strategic advantage — using something in a way that multiplies its value.

Word Family

  • Leverage (verb) — to use strategically
  • Leverage (noun) — the advantage itself ("We have leverage in this negotiation")
  • Leverageable — not a real word, please don't use it

Alternatives

Leverage → capitalize on, build upon, harness, take advantage of, make the most of, draw on Pick the one that fits the tone: Formal report → "capitalize on" Casual Slack → "make the most of" Presentation → "build upon"

front: Conditional: 'Were we to...' (Formal Inversion)
back:
What It Is

A formal way to express hypothetical situations by inverting the subject and auxiliary verb. It replaces "if" with a more elegant structure.

Pattern

Standard: "If we were to launch..." Formal: "Were we to launch..." Standard: "If we had known..." Formal: "Had we known..." Standard: "If it should happen..." Formal: "Should it happen..."

Examples in Context

Design & product scenarios
  • "Were we to launch without testing, we would risk significant user drop-off and increased support costs."
  • "Had we known about the API constraint earlier, we could have adjusted the timeline and avoided the crunch."
  • "Should the stakeholders decide to expand scope, we'll need to revisit the resource allocation."
  • "Were this feature to ship in its current state, we'd likely see a spike in error reports."

When to Use It

Context guide
  • ✅ Formal presentations to leadership
  • ✅ Written proposals and strategy docs
  • ✅ Executive communications and emails to C-suite
  • ✅ Design rationale documents
  • ❌ Slack messages (sounds stiff)
  • ❌ Daily standups (too formal)
  • ❌ 1:1s with your manager (feels distant)

Why It Works

Formal inversion adds rhetorical weight to hypotheticals. It signals that you've thought carefully about consequences. In a presentation, "Were we to delay launch by two weeks" sounds more deliberate than "If we delayed." Use sparingly — overuse makes everything sound like a legal brief.

front: How to open a design presentation with impact
back:
The Rule

Start with the problem, not the solution. If people don't care about the problem, they won't care about your design.

Three Hook Types

Pick one to open with
  1. Data hook:
    "Last month, 34% of our users abandoned the onboarding flow at step 3. That's 12,000 potential customers we lost."
  2. Story hook:
    "I watched a user try to complete this task — it took her 7 attempts and she almost gave up. Let me show you why."
  3. Question hook:
    "What would it mean for our business if we could cut onboarding time in half?"

The Bridge

After your hook, connect to your solution: "Today I'll walk you through how we might solve this." This creates a narrative arc: problem → investigation → solution → impact.

Never Start With

Weak openings to avoid
  • ❌ "So I've been working on some wireframes..." — no context for why anyone should care
  • ❌ "This is still rough but..." — undermines your work before you even show it
  • ❌ "I'm not sure if this is right, but..." — signals lack of conviction
  • ❌ "Let me just walk you through the screens..." — a tour, not a story
  • ❌ "Before I start, some disclaimers..." — never apologize before presenting

Full Opening Example

Putting it all together

"[Data hook] Our checkout abandonment rate jumped 15% last quarter. The biggest drop-off is at the payment step — users start entering info and leave.

[Bridge] We ran 8 usability sessions to understand why, and what we found surprised us. Today I'll share three design changes that address the root cause.

[Preview] I'll walk through the problem, our approach, and the expected impact on conversion."

Total time: 30 seconds. Now everyone is paying attention.

front: Hedging: Softening statements in meetings
back:
What Hedging Is

Hedging lets you express opinions without sounding dogmatic or absolutist. It signals intellectual honesty — you're sharing your view while leaving room for other perspectives.

Hedging Phrases

Useful in discussions
  • "Based on what we've seen so far..."
  • "My initial take is..."
  • "I'd lean towards... but I'm open to other perspectives."
  • "One way to think about this might be..."
  • "The data seems to suggest..."
  • "From a design standpoint, I'd argue..."
  • "If the research is directionally correct..."

When to Hedge vs. When to Be Direct

HEDGE when: BE DIRECT when: ────────── ───────────── Early discussions Final recommendations Incomplete information Known answers to direct Qs Small sample sizes Strong evidence Sensitive/cross-team topics Your area of expertise Exploring options Presenting decisions "I think we might consider..." "We should do X because..."

The Goldilocks Zone

Finding the right level

Over-hedging (too soft):
"I could be wrong, but maybe possibly we might want to perhaps consider looking at the data, if that's okay..."
Sounds unsure and untrustworthy.

Under-hedging (too blunt):
"This is clearly the wrong approach and we need to change it immediately."
Sounds rigid and dismissive.

Just right:
"Based on what the data is showing, I'd recommend we reconsider this approach. Here's why..."
Confident but open.

Golden Rule

Match your certainty to your evidence. Strong data = strong language. Weak data = hedged language. This builds trust because people know when you're confident, you have reason to be.

front: Iterate (verb)
back:
Definition

To repeat a process with the goal of improvement. Each cycle incorporates what was learned in the previous one. It's the heartbeat of design.

In Context

Professional usage
  • "We iterated on the wireframes based on user feedback from three rounds of testing."
  • "After three iterations, the conversion rate improved by 22%."
  • "Let's ship this version and iterate based on real usage data."
  • "The first iteration was rough, but each cycle got us closer to the right solution."

Word Family

  • Iterate (verb) — to go through a cycle of improvement
  • Iteration (noun) — one cycle ("This is iteration 3")
  • Iterative (adjective) — describing the process ("an iterative approach")
  • Iteratively (adverb) — "We refined the design iteratively"

Iterate vs. Repeat

Iterate ≠ Repeat Iterate: v1 → learn → v2 → learn → v3 Each version incorporates feedback Repeat: v1 → v1 → v1 → v1 Same thing over and over No learning between cycles
Key distinction

If you're not learning between cycles, you're not iterating — you're repeating. The value of iteration is in the feedback loop, not the repetition.

Related Terms

Iterate = improve through cycles. Pivot = fundamentally change direction. Refine = polish within the same direction. Know the difference — stakeholders use these interchangeably, but they mean very different things for scope and timeline.

front: Parallel Structure in Lists
back:
The Rule

When listing items, keep the same grammatical form. This makes your writing clearer, more rhythmic, and more professional.

Wrong vs. Right

Broken parallelism

"The role involves designing interfaces, user research, and to write documentation."
(gerund, noun, infinitive — inconsistent!)

Parallel structure

"The role involves designing interfaces, conducting user research, and writing documentation."
(all gerunds — parallel!)

More Examples

Fixing parallelism

❌ "We need to move fast, be precise, and having good communication."
✅ "We need to move fast, be precise, and communicate well."

❌ "The design should be clean, usable, and it should feel modern."
✅ "The design should be clean, usable, and modern."

Where It Matters Most

Bullet points in presentations → Each bullet starts with same form ✅ "Reduced load time by 40%" ✅ "Increased conversion by 12%" ✅ "Decreased support tickets by 25%" ❌ "Reduced load time by 40%" ❌ "12% increase in conversion" ❌ "Support tickets went down"

Applies To

  • Bullet points in presentations and docs
  • Resume descriptions — especially impactful here
  • Any list in professional writing
  • Comparisons using "both...and", "either...or", "not only...but also"

Parallelism makes your writing clearer, more rhythmic, and more professional. (See the pattern?)

front: How to handle 'I don't know' in a meeting
back:
The Rule

Never bluff — it erodes trust when discovered. And it will be discovered. Admitting uncertainty with a plan is far more professional than making something up.

Professional Alternatives

Scripts you can use
  • "That's a great question — I don't have the data in front of me, but I'll follow up by end of day."
  • "I'd want to validate that assumption before committing. Let me check and get back to you by Thursday."
  • "My instinct says [X], but I'd want to confirm with [data source]."
  • "I know the general direction, but let me get the specific numbers and send a follow-up."
  • "That's outside my area of expertise — [person] would know. Let me loop them in."

Key Rules

  • Always specify WHEN you'll follow up — vague promises are worse than silence
  • It's okay to share your instinct while flagging it's unverified
  • "I don't know yet" is better than "I don't know" — the word "yet" implies action
  • Follow through — if you say you'll get back by Thursday, do it by Wednesday

Why This Builds Credibility

The trust equation

People trust someone who's honest about gaps far more than someone who always has an answer. When you admit you don't know something, it makes all the things you do claim carry more weight.

Conversely, getting caught bluffing once makes people question everything you've said before.

The Worst Responses

❌ Bluffing with made-up numbers ❌ "I think it's around... maybe... 40%?" ❌ Deflecting: "That's really more of a PM question" ❌ "I don't know" with no follow-up plan ❌ Silence / hoping they move on

front: Synthesize (verb)
back:
Definition

To combine multiple pieces of information into a coherent, new understanding. It's not summarizing (making shorter) — it's creating insight by connecting dots.

In Context

Professional usage

"I synthesized findings from 12 user interviews into three key themes that informed our design direction."

"After synthesizing the survey data, analytics, and support tickets, a clear pattern emerged."

Synthesize vs. Summarize

Summarize = shorten existing info "Here's a shorter version" Input: 10 pages → Output: 1 page Synthesize = create NEW understanding by connecting pieces Input: 3 data sources → Output: insight that NO single source contained

Example of Synthesis

Connecting disparate data

"The survey said users wanted faster load times. The usability tests showed they weren't bothered by actual speed. Support tickets revealed frustration with 'not knowing what's happening.'"

Summary: Users complained about speed, tests showed speed was fine, support got complaints.

Synthesis: The real issue isn't speed — it's perceived speed. Users need better loading indicators and progress feedback. The problem is uncertainty, not performance.

Neither data source alone told this story. The insight emerged from combining them.

When to Use This Word

  • After research: "synthesizing interview findings"
  • In design reviews: "synthesizing feedback from three stakeholders"
  • In presentations: "Here's what I synthesized from the data"

Related: distill, integrate, consolidate, make sense of

front: Closing a presentation with a clear call-to-action
back:
The Rule

End with what you need from the audience. A presentation without a CTA is a lecture. You showed the work — now tell them what to do with it.

Strong Closings

Scripts that work
  • "I'd love your alignment on [specific decision] so we can move to implementation this sprint."
  • "The next step is [specific action] — are we aligned?"
  • "I'm looking for a go/no-go on [specific thing] today."
  • "What questions do you have before we commit to this direction?"
  • "I need [specific resource/decision] to move forward. Can I get that by [date]?"

What to Avoid

❌ Weak closings ✅ Strong closings ────────────── ─────────────── "So yeah, that's it." "Are we aligned on X?" "Any thoughts?" "What Qs before we commit?" "I guess we'll figure out" "I need Y by Friday." "Questions?" [Q&A slide] [CTA slide] then questions

The Order Matters

CTA before Q&A

State your ask before opening for questions. Otherwise the discussion may drift and you lose your moment. The last thing people hear is what they remember.

Flow:

  1. Recap your recommendation (10 seconds)
  2. State your CTA clearly
  3. "Now — what questions do you have?"

Full Closing Example

Putting it together

"To recap: we've identified that users abandon checkout due to unclear shipping costs. Our proposed solution — a real-time shipping calculator — tested well with 8 out of 10 participants.

I'm looking for approval to move this into development this sprint. Engineering has estimated 5 story points, and we'd be ready to A/B test by the 15th.

What questions do you have?"

front: Cleft Sentence: 'What... is...' for Emphasis
back:
What It Is

A sentence structure that splits (cleaves) a normal sentence to emphasize a specific element. It adds rhetorical weight without raising your voice.

The Pattern

Normal sentence: "We need more user research." Cleft sentence: "What we need is more user research." └─ emphasized element ─┘

Examples in Design Contexts

Normal → Cleft
  • "We need more user research."
    → "What we need is more user research."
  • "Early alignment with engineering made this successful."
    → "What made this successful was early alignment with engineering."
  • "I'm proposing a phased rollout."
    → "What I'm proposing is a phased rollout."
  • "Users want clarity, not more features."
    → "What users want is clarity, not more features."
  • "The data tells us we have a retention problem."
    → "What the data tells us is that we have a retention problem."

When to Use It

  • Drawing attention to a key point in a heated discussion
  • Adding rhetorical weight in presentations (especially conclusions)
  • Redirecting a conversation: "What we should focus on is..."
  • Reframing the discussion: "What this really comes down to is..."

Other Cleft Forms

Variations

It-cleft: "It was the onboarding flow that caused the biggest drop-off."

All-cleft: "All we need is two more days of testing."

The thing-cleft: "The thing that concerns me is the lack of error handling."

Use sparingly — one or two per presentation. Overuse makes everything sound dramatic.

front: Mental Model
back:
Definition

A mental model is a user's internal belief about how a system works. It is built from past experiences, intuitions, and observations — not from the actual logic inside the product. When a product's design matches the user's mental model, it feels intuitive. When it doesn't, users feel lost or frustrated.

Mental Model vs. Implementation Model

User's Mental Model → what they EXPECT to happen Design Model → what the designer intended Implementation Model → how the system ACTUALLY works Goal: align all three as closely as possible

As a designer, your job is to bridge the gap between the user's mental model and how the system truly behaves. You do this through clear visual language, familiar patterns, and good feedback.

Real-World Example

Email trash folder

Most users believe that deleting an email sends it to a "Trash" folder where it stays forever until they manually empty it. The actual system auto-deletes after 30 days. Because the design shows a trash can icon and lets users "restore" emails, it successfully matches the mental model — even if the underlying logic is time-limited.

Why It Matters for Product Design

When onboarding new users, always design for the mental model they arrive with, not the one you wish they had. Conduct user research to discover what metaphors and expectations your audience already holds. Then use familiar UI patterns, consistent terminology, and clear feedback to reinforce a correct mental model over time.

front: Usability Testing
back:
Definition

Usability testing is a research method where real users attempt to complete tasks with a product while a researcher observes and takes notes. The goal is to discover where users struggle, get confused, or fail — so the team can fix problems before launch. It is one of the most direct ways to validate design decisions with evidence.

Common Formats

  • Moderated — a facilitator guides the session in real time, asking follow-up questions
  • Unmoderated — users complete tasks independently via a tool like Maze or UserTesting.com
  • Think-aloud protocol — users narrate their thoughts as they interact, revealing hidden confusion
  • Remote vs. in-person — remote is faster and cheaper; in-person captures richer non-verbal cues

Example Session

Task prompt example

"You just downloaded our app. Without any help from me, please find and purchase the cheapest flight from London to Tokyo."

The researcher silently observes, noting hesitations, wrong taps, and moments of confusion. Afterwards, they ask: "What did you expect to happen when you tapped that button?"

How Many Users Do You Need?

Jakob Nielsen's research found that 5 users uncover roughly 85% of usability problems in a single round of testing. More rounds with fresh users are more valuable than one large session. Aim for small, frequent tests throughout the design process rather than one big test at the end.

front: "Delightful UX" — what does it really mean, and when does it help vs. hurt?
back:
What People Mean by It

"Delightful UX" refers to small, unexpected moments that make using a product feel enjoyable, human, or even fun. It is often used in design reviews and portfolios to describe micro-interactions, animations, playful copy, or surprises that go beyond mere functionality. The term is popular but frequently misused.

Examples of Genuine Delight

Real delight moments
  • Duolingo's streak animations — a fire icon grows and bounces when you maintain a learning streak, reinforcing habit formation
  • Slack's loading messages — rotating witty tips replace a boring spinner, reducing perceived wait time
  • Mailchimp's high-five screen — a cartoon hand appears before you send a campaign, acknowledging the nervousness of the moment
  • Pull-to-refresh animations — custom illustrations that match the brand's personality

When Delight Becomes a Problem

Delight that gets in the way of usability is not delight — it is decoration. An animation that delays a critical action, a quirky error message that obscures what went wrong, or a surprise that confuses first-time users all cause frustration, not joy. Delight should layer on top of a solid foundation, never replace it.

The Designer's Reality Check

Before adding a delightful moment, ask: Does this serve the user's goal, or only the designer's ego? Real delight is contextual — it appears at the right emotional moment (completing a task, recovering from an error, reaching a milestone) and disappears once it becomes familiar. Prioritize clarity and speed first; add delight as a finishing touch.

front: The HEART Framework
back:
What Is It?

HEART is a user-centered metrics framework developed by Google to measure the quality of a user experience at scale. It was created by Kerry Rodden, Hilary Hutchinson, and Xin Fu in 2010. The framework helps teams move beyond simple click counts and think about experience quality in a structured way.

The Five Dimensions

H — Happiness (How satisfied are users? Surveys, NPS, CSAT) E — Engagement (How often and deeply do users interact?) A — Adoption (Are new users starting to use a feature?) R — Retention (Are users coming back over time?) T — Task Success (Can users complete key tasks efficiently?)

For each dimension, teams define Goals, Signals, and Metrics — a sub-process often called the Goals-Signals-Metrics process.

Applied Example

Redesigning a checkout flow
  • Happiness: Post-purchase satisfaction survey score ≥ 4.2/5
  • Engagement: Average number of items added to cart per session
  • Adoption: % of new users who complete their first purchase within 7 days
  • Retention: % of buyers who return within 30 days
  • Task Success: Checkout completion rate, average time to complete

When to Use It

HEART is especially useful when a product team is launching a new feature or redesigning an existing one and needs to agree on what success looks like before building. It prevents teams from optimizing for only one number (like click rate) while ignoring negative side effects on other dimensions. Choose 2–3 dimensions most relevant to your current goal rather than tracking all five at once.

front: Friction (in UX)
back:
Definition

Friction refers to any point in a user experience that slows down, confuses, or discourages a user from completing an action. It is a deliberate metaphor borrowed from physics — just as physical friction resists motion, UX friction resists user progress. Friction can be accidental (a design mistake) or intentional (a deliberate design choice).

Accidental vs. Intentional Friction

  • Accidental friction — too many form fields, unclear error messages, slow load times, confusing navigation. Always bad. Should be eliminated.
  • Intentional friction — a confirmation dialog before deleting an account, a CAPTCHA to prevent bots, a mandatory review step before a financial transaction. Can be valuable when it prevents costly mistakes.

Example

Onboarding friction

A fitness app asks new users to enter height, weight, age, fitness goals, dietary preferences, and connect a wearable — all before showing any value. This is accidental friction: it delays the user's first rewarding moment. A better approach: ask only for the one piece of data needed to show a personalized result immediately, then collect the rest progressively.

The Designer's Rule of Thumb

Always ask: Is this friction protecting the user, or just making our job easier? Long forms that collect data for marketing purposes are friction that serves the business, not the user. Remove every step that doesn't directly help the user reach their goal. Reduce accidental friction ruthlessly. Add intentional friction only when the cost of a mistake is high.

front: "MVP" (Minimum Viable Product) — what it really means vs. how it's misused
back:
The Original Definition
